title: Comparison Operators
description: Greater than, less than, and not equal to.

slides.title: Beyond Equality ⚖️
slides.content:
# More than just equals

We know how to use `=` (equals). But what if we want items that cost *more* than $10? Or users who are *under* 18 years old?

We use Mathematical Comparison Operators!

slides.title: The Main Symbols 🧮
slides.content:
## Your arsenal

* `>` (Greater than)
* `<` (Less than)
* `>=` (Greater than or equal to)
* `<=` (Less than or equal to)

Example: `SELECT name FROM products WHERE price > 50;`

quizData.question: Which query correctly finds all users who are strictly OLDER than 21?
quizData.explanation: The > symbol means 'strictly greater than'. If we used >=, it would include 21-year-olds.

slides.title: Not Equal To 🙅‍♂️
slides.content:
## Avoiding data

Sometimes you want everything *except* a specific value. To say "Not Equal To", you use both arrows facing each other: **`<>`**.
*(Note: Some databases also accept `!=`, but `<>` is the universal SQL standard).* 

`SELECT * FROM users WHERE country <> 'USA';` (Get everyone who is NOT from the USA).

quizData.question: What does this query do: `SELECT * FROM movies WHERE genre <> 'Horror';`

options.text: It returns all movies that are NOT Horror movies

quizData.explanation: The <> symbol represents 'Not Equal To'. It filters out rows that match the condition.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Find the `title` of all books where the `pages` count is less than or equal to (`<=`) 300.
